title: Indiana sugar cream pie
subtitle: A Hoosier cream pie that sets without eggs
excerpt: Sugar cream pie is the state pie of Indiana: a filling of double cream, sugar, flour, and butter, poured raw into an unbaked or blind-baked crust and stirred partway through baking to prevent a skin from forming. It contains no eggs. The result is rich, dense, and very sweet, with a faint caramel note from the browned butter.
sourceNotes: Sugar cream pie is documented in Indiana and Midwestern American household cookbooks from the nineteenth century, with roots in Quaker and Shaker baking traditions in the region. Public-domain recipes appear in community cookbooks from the late 1800s and early 1900s.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pie must cool and set for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight. It keeps in the fridge for 3 to 4 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 180 degrees C for blind bake; reduce to 175 degrees C (conventional) for the filling

glossaryTerms.definition: Sugar cream pie sets through a combination of starch gelatinisation and fat crystallisation rather than egg protein coagulation. The flour stirred into the cream thickens the filling as the temperature rises. The pie must be stirred once or twice during baking to prevent a thick skin forming on the surface and to ensure the starch distributes evenly; without this stirring the filling sets unevenly. The pie is not fully set when it comes out of the oven; it firms as it cools over 4 or more hours.

content.text: Sugar cream pie is unusual: no eggs, no baking powder, no yeast. The filling is cream, sugar, and flour, poured raw into the pastry case. The starch in the flour thickens the filling as it bakes, but the

requires patience: the pie needs to come out of the oven still soft, then cool for several hours. The top is dusted with cinnamon and nutmeg before baking, which bakes in and gives the surface a warm, spiced aroma.

Rub the butter into the flour and salt until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Add cold water a tablespoon at a time until the dough just comes together. Wrap and chill for 30 minutes. Preheat the oven to 200 degrees C (conventional). Roll to line a 23 cm pie dish. Line with baking paper, fill with baking beans, and blind-bake for 15 minutes. Remove the beans and paper and bake for 5 more minutes until pale golden. Reduce the oven to 175 degrees C (conventional).

Whisk the double cream, caster sugar, flour, vanilla, and a pinch of salt in a bowl until the flour is dissolved and there are no lumps. Pour into the warm pastry case. Dot the surface with the cubed butter. Dust with the ground nutmeg and cinnamon. Bake for 20 minutes. Open the oven, gently stir the top layer of the filling with a spoon (do not disturb the pastry), and bake for a further 25 to 30 minutes until the top is golden and the filling jiggles like just-set jelly when moved. It will look very soft. Remove and leave to cool completely on a rack, at least 4 hours. Refrigerate once cool.
